- there is no timing issue, two parallel requests are deduped if they are for "the same module"

the example I have in mind is "you can only load this JS file as a script" which seems pretty reasonable?
if the module is already cached then there shouldn't be a need for another one
Because their cache is (URL, type) and not just URL
Because the type affects the headers
Because they want it to affect them
the feature for importing as text, or as bytes, is the language saying that no, that's not reasonable
uh
I... do not take the feature to mean that
can you explain why you'd only want a JS file to load as a script?

content.body: like what use case do you have where it's fine that they read the text with fetch, but not with import?
`fetch` does not and cannot have a `sec-fetch-dest` of `script`, so it is also a blocker for `fetch`
content.formatted_body: fetch does not and cannot have a sec-fetch-dest of script, so it is also a blocker for fetch

content.body: ah, ok, TIL
and there's no way with service workers to read it as text?
(the `sec-` prefix means you can't set it from a script by any means)
content.formatted_body: (the sec- prefix means you can't set it from a script by any means)

content.body: (obv there's always "another server" that can get it as text and proxy it down)
I think you could register a service worker which could read it as text if you are in a position to do so, but there are plenty of applications which don't allow you to register a service worker

either way i'd rather "this file can only be loaded as a script" be a header sent by the server, where the browser chooses how to obey it, than have the server change the response based on how it's being imported
(which also aligns better with the web's philosophy of "the user and user agent is in control, not servers"
* (which also aligns better with the web's philosophy of "the user and user agent is in control, not servers")

content.body: well, `sec-fetch-dest` already exists, is already `script` for `import` and `<script>`, and is already _not_ `script` for `fetch`
content.formatted_body: well, sec-fetch-dest already exists, is already script for import and <script>, and is already not script for fetch

content.body: so because there's a pre-existing bad design, it must be continued?
so your ask is, we already have this perfectly good mechanism which enforces this invariant, and we should stop doing so because you want servers not to have this power?
because it's not perfectly good
it delegates control to the server instead of the user, which contradicts HTML philosophy
I don't know what philosophy this is referring to. generally the web platform does not seek to deny information from servers
https://www.w3.org/WAI/UA/WD-UAAG10-TECHS-20010604/guidelines.html#gl-content-access and a few other guidelines in that doc, for one
the underlying principles of all the "Ensure user control" items is that the user should always be in charge
the _user_ can still view the script as a string, it's just the _web application_ that doesn't get to
content.formatted_body: the user can still view the script as a string, it's just the web application that doesn't get to

content.body: "view source" still works fine

Except that browsers already enforce strict mime type for things like scripts, so you can't run a script whose header is text/plain and the only real workaround is to change the mime type on the server.

* Except that browsers already enforce strict mime type checking for things like scripts, so you can't run a script whose header is text/plain and the only real workaround is to change the mime type on the server.

m.new_content.body: Except that browsers already enforce strict mime type checking for things like scripts, so you can't run a script whose header is text/plain and the only real workaround is to change the mime type on the server.

content.body:
also, scripts are not "content" in the sense meant by this guideline:

> In this specification, the noun "content" is used in three ways:
> It is used to mean the document object as a whole or in parts.
> It is used to mean the content of an HTML or XML element, in the sense employed by the XML 1.0 specification ([XML], section 3.1): "The text between the start-tag and end-tag is called the element's content." Context should indicate that the term content is being used in this sense.
> It is used in the context of the phrases non-text content and text content.
content.formatted_body:
also, scripts are not "content" in the sense meant by this guideline:

In this specification, the noun "content" is used in three ways:
It is used to mean the document object as a whole or in parts.
It is used to mean the content of an HTML or XML element, in the sense employed by the XML 1.0 specification ([XML], section 3.1): "The text between the start-tag and end-tag is called the element's content." Context should indicate that the term content is being used in this sense.
It is used in the context of the phrases non-text content and text content.

content.body: These are security invariants that are important to protect against bad actors attempting to smuggle in scripts under unexpected mime types

i understand the current reality, and it is perfectly reasonable to prevent unexpected execution of code. that doesn't mean the current reality needs to be extended to new language features, and in this case, i can smuggle in scripts and eval them just fine with or without the behavior i want
this mechanism isn't preventing that, it's just preventing me from being able to reliably inspect a resource that the browser otherwise executes/renders
you already do not have that ability, so it's a request to specifically _enable_ you do to do this thing which you could never previously do
content.formatted_body: you already do not have that ability, so it's a request to specifically enable you do to do this thing which you could never previously do

content.body: iow imo this _allows_ more smuggling in of code - because the web application can't inspect code before executing it.
content.formatted_body: iow imo this allows more smuggling in of code - because the web application can't inspect code before executing it.

content.body: I don't think we should have that as a goal of this feature
i mean i can already do it with fetch or other means _unless_ the server doesn't want me to, right?
content.formatted_body: i mean i can already do it with fetch or other means unless the server doesn't want me to, right?

content.body: right but you can also do it with this feature unless the server doesn't want you to
the requests made with this feature look basically like the requests made by `fetch`
content.formatted_body: the requests made with this feature look basically like the requests made by fetch

content.body: for the `bytes` proposal they look _exactly_ like those requests IIUC
content.formatted_body: for the bytes proposal they look exactly like those requests IIUC

content.body: hm
so i think my objection isn't so much that servers can deny inspection, it's more that they can lie - meaning you can choose to send a different module
* so i think my objection isn't so much that servers can deny inspection, it's more that they can lie - meaning you can choose to send different code

m.new_content.body: so i think my objection isn't so much that servers can deny inspection, it's more that they can lie - meaning you can choose to send different code

content.body: sending different data in response to different requests isn't lying
You could have a web page that serves HTML, CSS, JS, and a picture all at the same URL :) Why waste URLs for the various things
i mean you could, but does anyone do that?
not inherently. but it certainly could be
Many sites do.
You can often have an endpoint that serves REST or WSL or HTML based on the Accepts header. It happens all the time with CMS systems
* You can often have an endpoint that serves REST or WSL or HTML based on the Accepts header. It happens all the time with CMS

m.new_content.body: You can often have an endpoint that serves REST or WSL or HTML based on the Accepts header. It happens all the time with CMS

content.body: it's pretty common to serve machine-readable data if the Accepts header asks for json
also I have seen at least a few sites serve markdown if the Accepts header asks for markdown, as an affordance for LLMs
true. but if that data doesn't conceptually match the non-JSON resource i'd call that "lying"
everything behind Cloudflare https://blog.cloudflare.com/markdown-for-agents/
content.formatted_body: everything behind Cloudflare https://blog.cloudflare.com/markdown-for-agents/

content.body: Besides, varying data by an Accepts header is no different than varying data by a Cookie header, and the latter is also used all the time to serve different content based on whether you're signed in.
i do think it's different

Accepts is intended to change the representation of a resource, and indeed a URL is intended to map to a single resource. that some CMS's abuse that to serve wildly different content at a URL doesn't change that
the real shame is disregard for a `Vary` header field, which AFAIK is mandated for distinct destinations
content.formatted_body: the real shame is disregard for a Vary header field, which AFAIK is mandated for distinct destinations
